# CAD / Design Engineer (External — Customer)

> External persona — an end user. Spends most of their working day inside CAD tools. Doesn't make the buying decision but powerfully influences it through preference, complaints, and informal channels.

## Snapshot

- **Title examples:** Mechanical Engineer, Design Engineer, Senior CAD Designer, Industrial Designer, Product Engineer.
- **Industry context:** Manufacturing, automotive, aerospace, medical devices, industrial equipment, consumer products, AEC firms.
- **Tools used 6–8 hours/day:** SolidWorks, NX, Creo, Inventor, AutoCAD, CATIA, Revit (in AEC), Fusion 360, plus Adobe (rendering, technical illustration), MS Office, occasionally simulation tools (ANSYS, Abaqus).
- **Tenure:** 3–25 years of engineering practice; many spent on the same workstation tools.

## Goals

- Get work done without waiting on the machine.
- Run large assemblies and detailed simulations without crashes or freezes.
- Have responsive viewport interaction even on complex models.
- Trust the workstation enough to leave a long render or simulation overnight.
- Keep their personal productivity flow uninterrupted by IT or procurement decisions made above their head.

## Frustrations / pains

- "Spinning beach ball" or stalls when rotating large assemblies.
- Simulation runs that crash 4 hours in.
- Thermal throttling on mobile workstations during sustained renders.
- Fan noise during long jobs.
- Discovering after a fleet refresh that the new SKU is worse for their specific workload than the previous one.
- IT-imposed configuration locks that prevent installing or updating ISV tools.
- Slow-to-quote configurations from procurement that delay project starts.

## Buying behavior

- **Influences but rarely decides.** Provides input to engineering manager and IT.
- **Strongest influence channel:** preference voiced through the engineering manager + escalations when current hardware is failing them.
- **Evaluation behaviors:** reads ISV-published recommended hardware; watches YouTube CAD-workstation reviews; asks peers; tests evaluation units when offered.
- **Loyalty triggers:** a workstation that "just works" for years; vendors who respond fast when something breaks.

## What they want from a workstation

- Consistent responsiveness on large assemblies and complex parts.
- Pro GPU with ECC memory and ISV-certified drivers (they know what GeForce vs Quadro/RTX difference does to their crash rate).
- Plenty of memory (32–128 GB depending on workload) and fast NVMe storage.
- Reliable thermal envelope under sustained load — quiet preferred.
- Multi-monitor support (typically 2–3 monitors).
- For mobile users: portability that doesn't force them to give up performance.

## What they don't want

- Consumer hardware repackaged as professional with a different sticker.
- Software bloat from preinstalled trials.
- Mandatory cloud sync on local CAD files.
- IT forcing the same SKU across the team regardless of workload.

## Common objections (or quiet preferences)

- "Just give me the same one I had before, but faster."
- "I tried a MacBook Pro M-series for renders — it's surprisingly fast, why aren't we using those?"
- "The mid-tier crashes too often on simulation; we need the top tier."
- "The configurator is too complicated; can someone just tell me which one to ask for?"

## Maps to archetypes

- Primary: **Performance-Maximizer** — wants the fastest path to a finished design.
- Secondary: **Pragmatist** — values reliability over novelty when forced to choose.
